objectives: By the end of the week, students will master adding and subtracting decimals using concrete place-value strategies. They will then transition to multiplying decimals by analyzing how scaling by tenths and hundredths affects the position of the decimal point in a product.
agenda: Monday: Unit 5, Lesson 3: Adding and Subtracting Decimals with Diagrams
Tuesday: Unit 5, Lesson 4: Adding and Subtracting Decimals
Wednesday: Unit 5, Lesson 5: Decimal Points in Products
Thursday: Unit 5, Lesson 6: Methods for Multiplying Decimals
languageObjectives: Students will explain their strategies for aligning place values using the terms tenths, hundredths, and thousandths.
Students will write sentences justifying the placement of the decimal point in a product based on whole-number multiplication patterns.

objectives: By the end of the week, students will extend their operational understanding of decimals to multiplication and division. They will use area diagrams and partial products to determine decimal products and connect these visual models to vertical calculation strategies. Students will then transition to division, applying partial quotients and long division algorithms to compute accurate whole-number and decimal quotients while maintaining place-value reasoning.
agenda: Monday: Unit 5, Lesson 7: Using Diagrams to Represent Multiplication
Tuesday: Unit 5, Lesson 8: Calculating Products of Decimals
languageObjectives: Students will justify orally and in writing how area diagrams and partial products visually represent the decomposition and multiplication of multi-digit decimals.

Students will explain orally and in writing what each step in a partial quotients or long division calculation represents, using precise mathematical terminology (dividend, divisor, partial quotients, quotient).

objectives: By the end of the week, students will deepen their understanding of decimal arithmetic by using base-ten diagrams and standard algorithms to add, subtract, and multiply decimals. They will relate visual chunking models to numerical steps to maintain precise place-value accuracy.
agenda: Monday: Unit 5, Lesson 3: Adding and Subtracting Decimals with Diagrams
Tuesday: Unit 5, Lesson 4: Adding and Subtracting Decimals
Wednesday: Unit 5, Lesson 5: Decimal Points in Products
Thursday: Unit 5, Lesson 6: Methods for Multiplying Decimals
languageObjectives: Students will justify orally and in writing why lining up the decimal point is necessary when adding or subtracting decimals.
Students will use precise place-value terminology (tenths, hundredths, thousandths) to explain the position of a decimal point in a product.

objectives: By the end of the week, students will showcase their academic growth on the English and Math MAP assessments. They will also transition into Unit 5, beginning their exploration of decimal arithmetic and base-ten representations in real-world contexts.
agenda: Wednesday: Unit 5 Launch! Lesson 1: Using Decimals in a Shopping Context
Friday: Unit 5, Lesson 2: Using Diagrams to Represent Addition and Subtraction
homework: Delta Math Week 38 (Light week due to MAP testing!)
languageObjectives: Students will read, interpret, and discuss real-world shopping contexts involving decimals.
Students will explain how visual diagrams represent the addition and subtraction of decimals.
Students will use precise mathematical place-value terminology (tenths, hundredths) to describe decimal numbers.

objectives: By the end of the week, students will finalize their understanding of fraction division and volume, demonstrate their mastery on the end-of-unit assessment, and begin exploring decimal arithmetic in real-world contexts as we launch Unit 5.
agenda: Thursday: Unit 5 Launch! Lesson 1: Using Decimals in a Shopping Context
Friday: Unit 5, Lesson 2: Using Diagrams to Represent Addition and Subtraction
languageObjectives: Students will explain their problem-solving strategies for volume and fractional lengths.
Students will read and interpret real-world contexts involving decimals.
Students will use precise mathematical terminology to discuss operations with decimals.

objectives: By the end of the week, students will finalize their understanding of fraction division and volume by solving real-world problems. They will also successfully complete their Math MCAS testing before beginning our new unit.
homework: Delta Math Week 36 (Light week due to MCAS!)
languageObjectives: Students will read and comprehend multi-step word problems to identify the necessary mathematical operations.
Students will explain their problem-solving strategies and justify their solutions.
Students will use precise terminology to describe dimensions and volume in real-world contexts.

objectives: By the end of the week, students will finalize their understanding of fraction division and volume by solving real-world problems. They will demonstrate their mastery of these concepts on the end-of-unit assessment before transitioning into our new unit.
languageObjectives: Students will read and comprehend multi-step word problems to identify the necessary mathematical operations.
Students will explain their problem-solving strategies and justify their solutions.
Students will use precise terminology to describe dimensions and volume in real-world contexts.

objectives: By the end of the week, students will apply their understanding of fraction division and volume to solve real-world problems. They will also successfully complete their Math MCAS testing.
homework: Delta Math Catchup Week (Light week due to MCAS!)
languageObjectives: Students will read and comprehend multi-step word problems to identify the necessary mathematical operations.
Students will explain their problem-solving strategies and justify their solutions.
Students will use precise terminology to describe dimensions and volume in real-world contexts.

objectives: By the end of the week, students will solve problems about the side lengths and area of rectangles, and the edge lengths and volume of rectangular prisms using fraction division. They will determine the volume of a rectangular prism by packing it with unit cubes with appropriate unit-fraction edge lengths and by multiplying the edge lengths.
agenda: Lesson 14: Fractional Lengths in Triangles and Prisms
languageObjectives: Students will explain their mathematical strategies for finding missing lengths.
Students will justify their reasoning about volume calculations.
Students will use precise terminology—including gaps and packed—to describe measuring and filling prisms.

objectives: By the end of the week, students will generalize a process for dividing a number by a fraction and use the fraction division algorithm to calculate quotients. They will also apply these concepts to solve geometric problems involving fractional lengths.
agenda: Lesson 10: Dividing by Unit and Non-Unit Fractions
Lesson 11: Using an Algorithm to Divide Fractions
languageObjectives: Students will interpret and explain how to represent situations involving division.
Students will justify their mathematical strategies for dividing fractions.
Students will use precise terminology—including numerator, denominator, and reciprocal—to explain their reasoning.

objectives: By the end of the week, students will be able to solve problems involving the division of fractions by using diagrams, writing equations, and reasoning about the relationship between multiplication and division. They will represent problems involving fractional divisors to answer questions like 'How many groups?' and 'How much in one group?'.
agenda: Lesson 6: Using Diagrams to Find the Number of Groups
languageObjectives: Students will justify their reasoning about division and tape diagrams orally and in writing.
Students will explain how to create and make sense of division diagrams.
Students will use precise terminology—including whole, equal-size, fraction of, and unknown—to interpret and represent division situations.

objectives: By the end of the week, students will be able to describe the relationship between the size of the divisor and the size of the quotient in division problems. They will represent division situations using equations like a ÷ b = a/b and diagrams to answer two fundamental questions: 'How many groups?' and 'How much in each group?'.
agenda: Lesson 1: Size of Divisor and Size of Quotient
Lesson 2: Meanings of Division (How many groups? vs. How much in a group?)
languageObjectives: Students will use the terms dividend, divisor, and quotient to describe parts of a division equation.
Students will interpret and represent division situations by creating tape diagrams and writing corresponding multiplication or division equations.
Students will justify their reasoning about division by explaining how a diagram represents either the number of groups or the size of each group.
